treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides early in the season
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
tomato_bacterial_spot.about: Bacterial spot is a devastating disease of tomatoes causing small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown and lead to defoliation. It also causes scabby, dark lesions on the fruit.

prevention: Stake plants to improve air flow
Water at the base of the plant
tomato_early_blight.about: Early blight causes dark, concentric rings (bullseye pattern) on older leaves. It often leads to yellowing of surrounding tissue and premature leaf drop.

tomato_healthy.about: The plant appears perfectly healthy with vibrant green foliage and no visible signs of pathogen or pest damage.

treatment: Infected plants must be destroyed immediately
tomato_late_blight.about: Late blight is highly destructive, causing large, dark, water-soaked lesions on leaves and stems, and firm, brown, irregular lesions on fruit.

prevention: Water at the base to keep foliage dry
tomato_septoria_leaf_spot.about: Septoria leaf spot presents as numerous small, circular spots with dark borders and gray centers on the lower leaves.

treatment: No cure exists for infected plants
Remove and destroy symptomatic plants to prevent spread
tomato_yellow_leaf_curl_virus.about: This viral infection causes severe stunting, upward curling and yellowing of leaf margins, significantly reducing fruit yield.

probable_cause: High rainfall and humidity during flowering/fruiting
treatment: Post-harvest hot water treatment of fruit
mango_anthracnose.about: Anthracnose is the most serious disease of mangoes, causing dark, irregular lesions on leaves, blossom blight, and sunken black spots on ripening fruit.

prevention: Prune out diseased branches during dry weather
treatment: Copper-based bactericides can suppress symptoms but not cure the tree
mango_bacterial_canker.about: Causes raised, angular, black lesions with water-soaked margins on leaves, and star-shaped cracking lesions on fruit and twigs.

probable_cause: Eggs laid in young leaf tissue
treatment: Systemic insecticides during new leaf flush
Prune and burn heavily galled leaves
mango_gall_midge.about: Larvae feeding inside the leaf tissue cause the formation of small, raised, pimple-like galls on the leaves, leading to leaf distortion and drop.

probable_cause: Cool, dry weather with high morning humidity
treatment: Apply sulfur or systemic fungicides at first sign of infection
mango_powdery_mildew.about: Characterized by a white, powdery fungal growth on leaves, inflorescences, and young fruit, often causing massive flower and fruit drop.

probable_cause: Capnodium species growing on honeydew excreted by sap-sucking insects (hoppers, mealybugs)
prevention: Control the primary insect pests (hoppers, scales, mealybugs)
treatment: Apply insecticidal soaps or neem oil to control insects
The mould will wash off once the insects are gone
mango_sooty_mould.about: Sooty mould is a black, crust-like coating on leaves that blocks sunlight and reduces photosynthesis, though it does not directly infect the plant tissue.

treatment: Store fruit at optimal cool temperatures
mango_alternaria.about: Alternaria rot (black spot) causes small black spots on the fruit surface that can enlarge into firm, sunken lesions as the fruit ripens.

probable_cause: High temperature and humidity during ripening
mango_black_mould_rot.about: A post-harvest disease causing a soft, watery rot on the fruit accompanied by the production of black, powdery spore masses.

probable_cause: Endophytic fungi that activate during fruit ripening
prevention: Harvest with a portion of the pedicel intact
mango_stem_end_rot.about: A major post-harvest disease where a dark, spreading rot begins at the stem end of the fruit as it ripens, eventually consuming the entire mango.

mango_healthy.about: The mango leaves or fruit appear completely healthy with no signs of fungal, bacterial, or pest damage.

probable_cause: Overwintering in dead bark, twigs, and mummified fruit
prevention: Remove dead wood and mummified fruit
Prune to open the tree canopy
treatment: Apply protective fungicides from silver tip stage to harvest
apple_black_rot.about: Black rot causes frogeye leaf spots, limb cankers, and a firm, brown-to-black rot on the fruit, often with concentric rings.

apple_healthy.about: The apple foliage is healthy, displaying vibrant green color and no structural damage.

probable_cause: Proximity to Eastern Red Cedar trees (alternate host)
prevention: Remove nearby cedar trees if possible
treatment: Apply protective fungicides during the period of spore release in spring
apple_rust.about: Cedar apple rust causes bright yellow-orange spots on apple leaves, which eventually develop small, tube-like structures on the underside.

prevention: Rake and destroy fallen leaves in autumn
treatment: Apply fungicides preemptively before rain events in spring
apple_scab.about: Apple scab appears as olive-green to black, velvety lesions on leaves and corky, scabby spots on the fruit, causing fruit deformation.

prevention: Harvest when vines are completely dead
potato_early_blight.about: Early blight causes dark, concentric ring spots on potato leaves and can also cause dark, sunken lesions on the tubers.

potato_healthy.about: The potato leaves are healthy and fully functional.

treatment: Destroy infected vines prior to harvest
potato_late_blight.about: The pathogen responsible for the Irish Potato Famine, causing rapid, destructive blighting of foliage and severe, reddish-brown granular tuber rot.

prevention: Water at the base, keeping leaves dry
Prune to open the bush center
treatment: Apply fungicidal sprays (e.g., neem oil, chlorothalonil)
Remove and dispose of infected leaves
rose_black_spot.about: Black spot causes circular black spots with fringed margins on leaves, leading to severe yellowing and defoliation of the rose bush.

rose_downy_mildew.about: Downy mildew causes irregular, purplish-red to dark brown spots on leaves, and a sparse, grayish fuzz may appear on the undersides. Defoliation is extremely rapid.

rose_healthy.about: The rose leaves are healthy, glossy, and free from pathogenic spots or mildews.

probable_cause: Warm days, cool nights, high humidity but dry leaf surfaces
treatment: Spray with horticultural oils, sulfur, or potassium bicarbonate
rose_powdery_mildew.about: Powdery mildew forms a white, powdery coating on new leaves, stems, and buds, causing the emerging foliage to become crinkled and distorted.

rose_rust.about: Rose rust causes small, bright orange pustules on the undersides of leaves, which later turn black. It can cause severe defoliation and weakened plants.

probable_cause: Prolonged high humidity and overcast days
prevention: Tillage to bury infected crop residue
treatment: Foliar fungicides applied at or shortly after tasseling
corn_gray_leaf_spot.about: Characterized by rectangular, tan to gray lesions that are restricted by the leaf veins, giving them a blocky appearance.

corn_healthy.about: The corn leaves are healthy, showing rich green color without lesions or blights.

treatment: Apply foliar fungicides if lesions appear early in the season
corn_northern_leaf_blight.about: This blight causes long, elliptical, cigar-shaped, grayish-green to tan lesions on the leaves.

probable_cause: Cool to moderate temperatures and high humidity
prevention: Plant resistant hybrids (the most effective method)
treatment: Fungicides may be used in severe cases on susceptible sweet corn varieties
corn_rust.about: Common rust causes small, oval, cinnamon-brown pustules on both upper and lower leaf surfaces, which release powdery spores.

treatment: Copper bactericide sprays can slow the spread
bell_pepper_bacterial_spot.about: Causes small, water-soaked, irregular spots on leaves that turn brown. Infected leaves often turn yellow and drop off.

bell_pepper_healthy.about: The bell pepper plant is healthy and free of disease.

probable_cause: Warm, wet weather during early season
prevention: Prune and destroy mummified fruit and infected canes
treatment: Fungicide applications from early shoot growth through fruit set
grape_black_rot.about: Black rot attacks leaves, causing tan spots with dark borders, but is most destructive to the berries, turning them into hard, black, shriveled mummies.

probable_cause: Phaeomoniella chlamydospora, Phaeoacremonium minimum, and wood-rotting basidiomycetes
prevention: Protect pruning wounds with wound sealants
treatment: There is no cure; infected vines or cordons must be removed and destroyed (trunk renewal)
grape_esca.about: Esca (Measles) causes characteristic 'tiger-stripe' patterns of necrosis and chlorosis on leaves, and can cause sudden vine collapse or wood rot.

grape_healthy.about: The grape foliage is healthy and robust.

grape_leaf_blight.about: Leaf blight causes irregular, reddish-brown spots that can coalesce, leading to premature defoliation and weakened vines.

strawberry_healthy.about: The strawberry plant is lush and completely healthy.

prevention: Keep plant canopy open for quick drying
treatment: Fungicidal sprays applied in early spring and post-harvest
strawberry_leaf_scorch.about: Leaf scorch causes numerous irregular, purplish-to-brown spots that coalesce, causing the leaf to look burned or 'scorched', curling upward at the edges.
